evals.prompt:
Review this PC/SC card reading code:
```python
from smartcard.System import readers
from smartcard.util import toHexString

GET_UID = [0xFF, 0xCA, 0x00, 0x00, 0x00]

def read_card_uid():
    r = readers()
    conn = r[0].createConnection()
    conn.connect()
    data, sw1, sw2 = conn.transmit(GET_UID)
    print(f'UID: {toHexString(data)}')
    conn.disconnect()
```
evals.expected_output: Analysis covering: (1) APDU [FF CA 00 00 00] — identifies this as GET DATA for UID (pseudo-APDU, handled by reader not card); (2) No SCARDCONTEXT — missing SCardEstablishContext, using pyscard's abstraction; (3) sw1/sw2 not checked — 90 00 = success, but other codes are ignored; (4) No exception handling for CardConnectionException (card removed mid-operation); (5) No SCardReleaseContext or resource cleanup on error paths.

assertions.text: Identifies [FF CA 00 00 00] as a GET UID pseudo-APDU
Flags that sw1/sw2 status bytes are not validated
Identifies missing exception handling for card removal

evals.expected_output: ATR parsing: 3B = direct convention, 8F = T=0, TA1=80 (Fi=372, Di=1, 372 etu), TB1 not present, TC1=80 (extra guard time), historical bytes starting at 80... The historical bytes 4F 0C A0 00 00 03 06 03 00 01 00 00 00 00 identify this as a specific card profile (likely a government or banking smart card with AID A0 00 00 03 06). TCK byte 6A is the XOR checksum.

assertions.text: Correctly identifies 3B as direct convention (not inverse)
Interprets the TA1=80 clock rate conversion factor
Identifies the historical bytes as containing an AID (A0 00 00 03 06)
